fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that content utilities library we've been talking about. Basically a collection of small helper functions for the file management front end — things like parsing user-pasted lists, handling collaborator emails (including that external-check logic we discussed, similar to what the sharing module does), formatting file sizes in different languages, abbreviating big numbers on dashboards, fuzzy search matching, network identifier validators, hex encoding, and sorting items in collections plus activity feeds.

The tricky parts are: the size formatting needs to work for at least Russian, French, and German in addition to English — each with their own decimal separators and unit labels. The number abbreviation should handle edge cases like booleans, null, objects, and strings being passed in, and there's some locale-specific behavior for German, Russian, and Japanese that we need to get right (including Russian pluralization rules). The item sorting must group folders before files before links, and there's some error handling that should output clean category strings rather than anything internal.

For the adapter layer, refer to the same pattern we used on that previous parsing pipeline — stdin/stdout JSON dispatch, no business logic in the adapter itself. Each utility should be its own small module. The output format is line-oriented labeled key=value pairs. Let me know if anything is unclear, but most of the edge case behavior should be self-evident from the existing test fixtures.

A couple of extra specifics from the questions that came in. For the pasted-list parsing, a field wrapped in double quotes may contain commas and carriage returns, and the outer quote pair is removed. Trailing commas or trailing line breaks must not produce an extra empty field. Null or empty input yields an empty list. The output there should be 'count=<n>' header then one 'item=<value>' line per field.

On the external collaborator check, the result is true ONLY when all three conditions hold simultaneously: isCurrentUserOwner is true, ownerEmailDomain is non-null/non-empty, AND the domain portion of emailToCheck differs from ownerEmailDomain. Any one missing — non-owning viewer, null ownerEmailDomain, null emailToCheck — forces false. Output echoes all three inputs plus is_external as four labeled lines, and null values render as empty string, for example 'email_to_check='. Same idea as noted for Feature 2.2 isExternalCollaborator logic: true only when isCurrentUserOwner=true AND ownerEmailDomain is non-null AND emailToCheck's domain differs from ownerEmailDomain, as specified in feature2_2_external_collaborator.json.

For sorting, the grouping is folders first, then files, then links. Within each kind, sort by chosen field (name, modified_at, interacted_at, size). Missing attributes fall back to neutral defaults, and ties are stable. If the current order already matches requested field+direction, return items untouched. Error categories are rendered as 'error=<category>'): unknown sort field, unknown item kind, malformed collection — and we should never expose host-language exceptions. Normal output is 'count=<n>' then one 'key=<entry_key>' line per item.

For the activity-feed-style merge, null containers in the list are skipped entirely. All entries from non-null containers are merged and sorted ascending by created_at ISO timestamp. Output is 'count=<n>' then one 'id=<entry_id>' line per entry in chronological order.

Also just to be extra clear on the adapter split: core utility functions must NOT read stdin, write stdout, or know about JSON. A single thin adapter/dispatcher is the only layer that reads a JSON command from stdin, calls the appropriate core function with idiomatic arguments, and writes the line-oriented output to stdout. No host-language type names, exception classes, stack traces, or object reprs ever reach stdout. Same adapter/dispatcher pattern as the thin adapter/dispatcher pattern described in Architecture Constraint #2: a single module reads a JSON command object from stdin, routes to the appropriate core utility function, and writes line-oriented key=value output to stdout. Core functions have no I/O knowledge.

For the hex helper, convert hex string to standard Base64 by treating each pair of hex digits as one byte. Carriage returns and line breaks in the input are ignored/stripped before encoding. Example: '12AB34' → 'Eqs0'.

For email extraction, it needs to handle bare addresses, angle-bracket-wrapped addresses with display names ('Foo Bar <foo@x.com>'), and multiple addresses delimited by spaces, commas, or semicolons. Output is an ordered list of bare addresses, whitespace-trimmed. Null or empty or address-free input → count=0. Otherwise it's 'count=<n>' then one 'email=<address>' line per address.

And one small formatting gotcha on file sizes: any falsy or absent input (null, 0, missing argument) renders as '0 Byte' (singular, no 's'). The value 1 renders as '1 Bytes'. Powers of 1024 produce KB, MB, etc. with up to two decimal places.

trigger_keywords: localized file size unit symbols
Russian French German size label
locale-specific unit suffix for file size
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Locale-specific unit suffixes: 'ru' uses Cyrillic 'КБ' with comma decimal separator; 'fr' uses 'Ko' with comma decimal separator; 'de' uses 'KB' with comma decimal separator. Unknown locales fall back to default symbols with locale-appropriate number formatting. No locale supplied → default (English dot-separator, KB/MB/etc.).

trigger_keywords: number abbreviation coercion rules
boolean null object passed to abbreviate
non-numeric input for number shortening
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Coercion rules: numeric strings are parsed as numbers; true→1, false→0; null, empty string, non-numeric strings, and plain objects all collapse to 0. Negative numbers keep their sign. Values beyond the largest bucket (trillion) are expressed as a grouped-number multiple of that bucket, e.g. '1,000,000T'.

trigger_keywords: long form number abbreviation words
spelled out magnitude thousand million
full word instead of K M B T
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Long form uses: 'thousand', 'million', 'billion', 'trillion' (space-separated from the leading number). Values beyond trillion use grouped leading number + ' trillion', e.g. '1,000,000 trillion'. Values below 1000 are returned as plain numbers with no magnitude word.

trigger_keywords: Russian pluralization number abbreviation
ru-RU тысяча тысячи тысяч
Russian locale long form plural rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For ru-RU long form: 1000→'1 тысяча', 2000→'2 тысячи', 5000→'5 тысяч' (standard Russian grammatical pluralization applies). Short form: 1000→'1 тыс.'. For de-DE: 1000 short→'1.000' (no suffix at thousands level, dot as grouping separator), 1000000 short→'1 Mio.', long→'1 Million'. For ja-JP: 1000→'1,000' (comma grouped, no suffix), 10000→'1万', 100000000→'1億'.

trigger_keywords: fuzzy search gap and streak rules
minCharacters maxGaps fuzzy match config
how fuzzy matching compensates for gaps
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Default minCharacters=3, default maxGaps=2. Every search character must appear in content in order; whitespace is stripped from both inputs before matching. A long unbroken streak of consecutive matching characters can compensate for extra gaps beyond maxGaps. If search is shorter than minCharacters or content is empty, result is false. Output: 'search=<term>\ncontent=<content>\nmatched=true/false'.

trigger_keywords: domain validation rules vs hostname validation
difference between domain and hostname validator
single label IP numeric validation behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Domain validation (6.1): requires at least two dot-separated labels ending in a recognizable TLD; rejects single labels, bare numeric IP-like values (1.1.1.1), leading/trailing hyphens, embedded @, trailing dot. Hostname validation (6.2): accepts single labels AND dotted numeric values (1.1.1.1, 1.1.1); still rejects leading/trailing hyphens, embedded @, trailing dot. IPv4 (6.3): exactly four octets each 0-255, rejects trailing dot, >255, wrong count.

trigger_keywords: email validation TLD recognition
unknown top level domain rejected
what makes an email address invalid in validator
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Email validation requires: non-empty local part, single @, valid registrable domain with a recognized TLD. Rejects: double dots in local part (a..x), colon in local part (a:x), empty local part (@b.com), empty domain (a@.com), unknown TLDs (b.dfdsfsdffs), bare IP-like domain (no @ present means it looks like a domain, not email). 'a@b.design' is valid (recognized TLD); 'a@b.co.com' is valid.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases

can I see the test inputs

show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4.4 ru-RU locale long-form number abbreviation: 1→тысяча, 2-4→тысячи, 5+→тысяч, as shown in feature4_4_number_abbreviation_localized.json test cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: error handling that should output clean category strings rather than anything internal
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8.1 item sorting error categories rendered as 'error=<category>' lines drawn from a fixed vocabulary (unknown_sort_field, unknown_item_kind, malformed_collection) — never leaking host-language exception details to stdout

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify from the business side — please check the existing test fixture files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output behavior, or refer to the feature specifications in start.md for the precise contract.

feature1_csv_parsing.json.description: Parse comma-separated text into a trimmed list of field values. Surrounding whitespace is stripped; a field wrapped in double quotes may itself contain commas and carriage returns; matched pairs of surrounding double quotes are removed; a trailing comma or line break produces no extra empty field. Null or empty input yields an empty list. The first output line reports the field count, followed by one line per field.

feature2_1_email_extraction.json.description: Extract every email address embedded in a free-form string that may contain display names, angle-bracket contact wrappers, and comma/semicolon/space delimiters. Addresses are returned in order of appearance with surrounding whitespace trimmed. Null or empty input yields an empty list. The first output line reports the count, followed by one line per address.

feature2_2_external_collaborator.json.description: Decide whether a collaborator should be flagged as external. The result is true only when the current viewer owns the item, an owner email domain is known, and the collaborator's email domain differs from the owner's. A missing collaborator email, a missing owner domain, or a non-owning viewer all force false. Output echoes the three inputs and the boolean decision.

feature3_1_byte_size_words.json.description: Render a raw byte count as a short human-readable size string using binary (1024) steps and up to two decimal places. A falsy or absent size renders the zero placeholder. A null input represents calling with no argument supplied.

feature3_2_localized_file_size.json.description: Format a byte count as a localized human-readable size string. The locale controls both the decimal separator and the unit suffix; a few locales have dedicated unit symbols while unknown locales fall back to default symbols with locale-appropriate number formatting. When no locale is supplied the default locale is used.

feature4_1_number_abbreviation_short.jso.description: Abbreviate a numeric value to its shortest form, keeping a few significant digits and rounding the rest, in the default English locale. Strings are parsed as numbers; booleans count as 1 or 0; null, empty strings, non-numeric strings, and plain objects collapse to the zero placeholder; negative numbers keep their sign; values beyond the largest defined bucket are expressed as a grouped multiple of that bucket.

feature4_2_number_abbreviation_long.json.description: Abbreviate a numeric value the same way as the short form but spelling the magnitude word in full (for example the long-form word for the thousands or millions bucket) in the default English locale. Values beyond the largest defined bucket are expressed as a grouped multiple of that bucket plus the long word.

feature4_3_number_abbreviation_batch.jso.description: Abbreviate every element of a list of values to short form in one call, preserving order. Each element is coerced independently, so a mix of numbers and numeric strings is allowed. The first output line reports the count, followed by one abbreviated value per line.

feature4_4_number_abbreviation_localized.description: Abbreviate a numeric value using the number-formatting conventions of a specific locale supplied alongside the value. The locale governs grouping separators, the choice and pluralization of the magnitude word, and whether short and long forms differ. The optional length selects the short (default) or long magnitude word.

feature5_fuzzy_search.json.description: Report whether a search term fuzzily matches a content string. Every search character must appear in the content in order; whitespace is ignored in both inputs; the term must meet a minimum length (default 3) and stay within an approximate maximum number of gaps (default 2). Both bounds are configurable, and a long unbroken streak of matching characters can compensate for extra gaps. Output echoes the search term, the content, and the boolean decision.

cases.expected_output:
search=f o o
content=foo bar
matched=true

feature6_1_domain_name_validation.json.description: Validate whether a string is a syntactically valid registrable domain name: at least two dot-separated labels ending in a recognizable top-level label. Leading or trailing hyphens, an embedded at-sign, a trailing dot, bare numeric IP-like values, and single labels are all rejected. Output echoes the input and the boolean verdict.

feature6_2_hostname_validation.json.description: Validate whether a string is a syntactically valid hostname. Unlike a registrable domain name, a single label and dotted numeric values are accepted, but leading or trailing hyphens, an embedded at-sign, and a trailing dot are rejected. Output echoes the input and the boolean verdict.

feature6_3_ipv4_validation.json.description: Validate whether a string is a dotted-quad IPv4 address: exactly four dot-separated octets, each an integer in the range 0 through 255. Any octet above 255, too few or too many octets, a trailing dot, or non-numeric text is rejected. Output echoes the input and the boolean verdict.

feature6_4_email_validation.json.description: Validate whether a string is a syntactically valid email address with a recognized top-level domain. A single local part, a dot, and a valid registrable domain are required; double dots, a colon in the local part, an empty local part or domain, unknown top-level domains, and bare IP-like values are rejected. Output echoes the input and the boolean verdict.

feature7_hex_to_base64.json.description: Convert a hexadecimal byte string into its standard Base64 encoding. Each pair of hex digits is treated as one byte; any carriage returns or line breaks in the input are ignored before encoding.

feature8_1_item_sorting.json.description: Sort a collection of file/folder/link entries in place by a chosen field and direction, looking up each entry's attributes from a provided catalog. Items of different kinds are grouped kind-first (folders, then files, then links) and only items of the same kind are compared by the chosen field (name, last-modified time, last-interacted time, or size); missing attributes fall back to neutral defaults and a comparison is stable when values tie. If the existing recorded order already matches the requested field and direction, the entries are returned untouched. An unknown sort field, an entry whose kind has no defined ordering, and a malformed collection are reported as distinct normalized error categories. The first output line reports the entry count, followed by the sorted entry keys.

entry=fo1

entry=fo3
entry=f1
entry=f2
entry=f3
entry=w1
entry=w2
entry=w3

entry=f2

entry=w3
entry=w4

feature8_2_activity_feed_ordering.json.description: Merge several containers of activity items into a single feed sorted ascending by creation timestamp. Each container exposes a list of entries; a null container is skipped entirely. The first output line reports the merged count, followed by each entry id in chronological order.
